title: Heap property and array layout

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Heap property and array layout; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Heap property and array layout
sources.claim: A binary heap is a complete tree whose parent priority dominates children, usually stored compactly in an array.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A binary heap is a complete tree whose parent priority dominates children, usually stored compactly in an array.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: In a zero-based heap, children of i are 2i+1 and 2i+2 when within size. Your goal is to trace parent-child indexes and validate a min- or max-heap. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Tree terminology and representation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A binary heap is a complete tree whose parent priority dominates children, usually stored compactly in an array. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Heap order is partial, not full sorting, and min versus max orientation changes the dominant element. The draft therefore rejects: Every level of a heap is globally sorted from left to right.

includes: Map complete binary tree relationships to array indexes and distinguish heap from sorted order.
Trace parent-child indexes and validate a min- or max-heap.

In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
excludes: Heap order is partial, not full sorting, and min versus max orientation changes the dominant element.

outcomes: Trace parent-child indexes and validate a min- or max-heap.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: A heap guarantees only parent-child priority and root extremum, not total order among siblings or levels.
